FULL CONTENT:
![](https://globalwaterforum.org/wp-content/uploads/2021/03/LOGO-PLUS-TEXT-100X500-300x60.png)

# Valuing urban park irrigation in a time of climate change

June 29th, 2022

![Share](https://static.addtoany.com/buttons/share_save_120_16.png)
![Print Friendly, PDF & Email](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAAAAAP///yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7)![Print Friendly, PDF & Email](https://cdn.printfriendly.com/buttons/printfriendly-pdf-email-button-notext.png)

![Print Friendly, PDF & Email](https://cdn.printfriendly.com/buttons/printfriendly-pdf-email-button-notext.png)

**Claire Doll, University of Western Australia, Perth, Australia**

*With increasing global exposure to water scarcity, cities are exploring different strategies to conserve water. One potential way to save water is to reduce irrigation of urban parks. Before changes to park irrigation practices are considered, it is important to understand how community benefits are linked to existing irrigation levels. Here **Claire Doll** provides highlights of a study she led on linking the value of park irrigation levels to the value of nearby homes. Irrigated areas of parks consistently add value to homes. Non-irrigated areas of parks also add value to homes classified as high-density dwellings.*

**A new lens for park valuation**

Urban parks provide a wide range of benefits to people (and especially to local residents). These include health, recreation, and environmental benefits like cooling and improvements to air quality. For these reasons, parks are heavily studied by environmental economists interested in estimating monetary-equivalent values of environmental amenities.

To date, most park valuation studies focus on estimating values in relation to the size and location of parks (Brander & Koetse, 2011). Differences in park values have also been considered based on the primary purpose of the park; for example, whether the park is for sporting purposes or nature reserves (Hatton Macdonald et al, 2010). However, despite over 50 years of work on park valuation, the links between park irrigation and park values remain unclear.

Our research explicitly examines the value of parks in relation to irrigation levels (Doll et al, 2022). This valuation work not only helps articulate the significance of parks to policy makers, but also helps inform land and water management decisions under climate change, declining groundwater levels, and increased exposure to water scarcity.

**From real estate sales to park values**

We look at the value of parks using the hedonic pricing method(Rosen, 1974). With this method, we use detailed housing information (eg, property size, number of bedrooms and bathrooms), proximity to different amenities (eg, schools), and proximity to environmental features (eg, the coast and urban parks) to explain the sales price of the house. By accounting for observable factors that we expect to influence sales prices, we can estimate how differences in what is offered at nearby parks contribute to the sales price of the house.

To better understand the relationships between park irrigation and park values, we connected with eight different local governments in the Perth metropolitan region for detailed administrative data on park irrigation. Park managers from these regions had information on how much of a park area was watered (typically using groundwater) because they are required to report this information to the Western Australia State water authority, the Water Corporation, as part of water licensing agreements.

We looked at differences between small parks (under 5 hectares) and large parks (5 hectares and over). Using the size of parks within a 250-meter radius zone surrounding a property, and the extent of park irrigation, we calculated a percentage area of small and large watered and non-watered park areas for each property in the study area. Our statistical analysis then led to monetary value estimates for these four types of park areas, across three types of housing densities.

**Park irrigation is important, but not always essential**

We found that the impacts of irrigation on the value of parks differ depending on the dwelling types of the nearby housing populations that they serve. Prices for all property types are influenced by the presence of watered park areas, and only apartment prices are influenced by the presence of non-watered park areas.

![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAAAAAP///yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7)
![](https://globalwaterforum.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/Fig-1a-800x449.jpg)
![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAAAAAP///yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7)
![](https://globalwaterforum.org/wp-content/uploads/2022/06/fig-1b-800x449.jpg)

**Imagining the future of park irrigation**

In most cases, the convention that parks have to be irrigated to deliver benefits to the public is supported. However, we find that non-irrigated park areas are also valued positively by nearby apartment dwellers. Accelerating rates of urbanization and shifts towards high-density living may support the development of more diverse park options that are less water-intensive. Increased visibility of these alternative park forms (consider figure 1), which could include more areas of native vegetation that do not require irrigation, may subsequently influence public expectations for landscape design that is more compatible with Perth’s changing climate and water future.

**More information**: [Claire.doll@uwa.edu.au](mailto:Claire.doll@uwa.edu.au)

**References**

Brander LM & MJ Koetse (2011). The value of urban open space: Meta-analyses of contingent valuation and hedonic pricing results. *J. Environ. Manage*. 92: 2763–2773.

Doll D, M Polyakov, DJ Pannell & MP Burton (2022). Rethinking urban park irrigation under climate change, *Journal of Environmental Management*,Volume 314, <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jenvman.2022.115012>

Hatton Macdonald D, ND Crossman, P Mahmoudi, LO Taylor, DM Summers & PC Boxall (2010). The value of public and private green spaces under water restrictions. *Landsc. Urban Plan*. 95: 192–200. <https://doi.org/10.1016/j.landurbplan.2010.01.003>

Rosen S (1974). Hedonic prices and implicit markets: product differentiation in pure competition. *J. Polit. Econ*. 82: 34–55.

**Claire Doll** is a PhD student in Agricultural and Resource Economics at the University of Western Australia. Her PhD research is centred on non-market valuation and benefit-cost analysis in the context of urban parks, native vegetation, and water use. She completed her BSc in Environmental and Conservation Sciences, majoring in Environmental Economics and Policy and MSc in Agricultural and Resource Economics at the University of Alberta in Edmonton, Canada.

The views expressed in this article belong to the individual authors and do not represent the views of the Global Water Forum, the UNESCO Chair in Water Economics and Transboundary Water Governance, UNESCO, the Australian National University, or any of the institutions to which the authors are associated. *Banner image: Cities looking to conserve water may look to cut back on irrigating urban parks. Decisions around such moves should consider the multiple values associated with those irrigation practices, including how it increases the values of homes near the park. enriched_points.expanded_content: A case study from San Isidro, Lima demonstrates the successful implementation of microirrigation systems, optimizing water use in urban parks while enhancing green space health.
Selecting drought-resistant plants not only conserves water but also ensures sustainable landscaping, as evidenced by studies showing improved resilience in urban environments.
Involving local communities in educational programs fosters awareness of water-efficient practices, encouraging public participation in irrigation management and sustainability initiatives.
